title: Jake Harmon
text:
The Journey
My journey into public health started as an EMT. When our fire department launched a Community Paramedicine program (designed to divert frequent, non-emergency callers from costly hospital trips) a colleague with her new MPH asked me to help evaluate it. While my sociology background was the initial hook, what really captured me was the data underneath. That experience showed me the power of systems thinking in public health, and it pushed me back to school to study epidemiology.
The past two years have been a tremendous learning experience. I’ve had the chance to work with a scintillating mosaic of public health stakeholders on high impact projects:

Stakeholder Collaboration


FederalStateLocalCommunity / NGOAcademic



CDC NCIRD — Survey collaboration and data dissemination with state and local health departments through CSTE partnership
CDC CNEIZD — Reviewing conditions for clean, regular data flows on enteric disease




Georgia Department of Public Health — Case interviewing (English/Spanish), disease surveillance via SENDSS
Georgia Environmental Protection Division — Multi-year air pollution trend analysis with university partners
Georgia Emerging Infections Program — Automating data cleaning for lab results on notifiable diseases with DPH




DeKalb County Fire Rescue — EMT response, community paramedicine program, opioid call support




Council of State and Territorial Epidemiologists — Workforce development, data modernization research, cost-effictiveness of federal dollars
Community Health Aligning Revitalization Reselience & Sustainability — Environmental health initiatives in West Atlanta
Emory Oaks — Autism support programming at Emory University
Southern Alliance for Public Health Leadership — VFC provider mapping and childhood immunization access analysis




Emory University — Data sharing agreement with CHARRS to support air pollution research in West Atlanta
University of Georgia — Data sharing agreement with CHARRS for cross-institutional air pollution analysis
Johns Hopkins University — Facilitated the “R for Trend Analysis” workshop at CSTE’s 2025 Annual Conference




In the spirit of “Ka Huina”, one of my most impactful partnerships was a project evaluating the state of the epidemiology workforce in the Pacific Territories and Freely Associated States. I spent a month conducting late-night, eastern time zone focus groups with Public Heath representatives from the Pacific to learn how CSTE could better support their activities, so, when I got to meet some of them in person at our 2025 Conference, it felt like seeing old friends.




Jake Harmon with Pacific Island epidemiologists at the CSTE Annual Conference

title: Jake Harmon
text:
The Work
Being a data-oriented person, I harness digital technologies to facilitate communication and teamwork. At CSTE, I created our organizations first-ever GitHub repository to improve collaborative analysis and to preserve our code for the future. Because many of my CSTE projects include sensitive data, however, I wanted to share some examples of other public-facing, communication projects I’ve created in order to give the team not just a sense of my breadth of experience, but to excite you with new ideas of how we could potentially engage our partners.




  
    

      
        
          Data Privacy & Governance
          K-Anonymity Simulation
          The project creates four progressive scenarios showing the delicate balance between making data publicly available and upholding HIPAA protections — with 3D Plotly visualizations that make the tradeoffs tangible.
          View on GitHub
          Watch Example Video
        
      

      
        
          Infectious Disease Modeling
          Influenza A-B Modeling Paper
          Coursework under Dr. Chowell at GSU, this document examines a two-strain influenza model using Bayesian statistics. Built as a fully reproducible research document — the kind of transparent workflow that builds trust and insight.
          View QMD Source
          Download Manuscript (.docx)
        
      

      
        
          Geospatial Analysis
          Vaccines for Children Map
          Built with the Southern Alliance for Public Health Leadership, this interactive Leaflet map examines the relationship between Vaccines for Children (VFC) provider locations and eligible child populations across Georgia and South Carolina.
          View Live Map (Georgia)
        
      

      
        
          Survival Analysis
          Contagious Canticles
          Using epidemiological methods to analyze Sacred Harp minutes data and understand how certain songs "catch on" and persist through singing communities.
          View Live App
          View on GitHub

title: Jake Harmon
text:
Why Kahuina
I love the pace and variety of Kahuina’s portfolio — pressing public health matters, work across different partners across different levels and sectors - and I’ve seen firsthand at CSTE how this kind of agility drives real innovation. From my research on how health departments are modernizing their data flows, I understand the future of epidemiology and informatics to be the same thing. I’m genuinely excited about the emerging technologies such as syndromic surveillance and wastewater surveillance, and I want to be part of forging this new horizon of public health.

title: Jake Harmon
text:
Why Jake Harmon
But beyond the work itself, what matters is how you do it. I’ve learned that collaboration isn’t just a nice-to-have, but rather how you build something that works. And I’ve learned that the difference between a “good solution” and a solution a partner will actually use often comes down to details. At CSTE, I composed parameterized reports housed in a GitHub repository because the partners needed version control and remote access. At DPH, I produced the same sort of reports as local, desktop-runnable files because we needed off-line, easily workable solutions. Same skill set, different execution. That’s what I bring: the attentiveness to understand what a partner actually needs, and the flexibility to deliver it right.
Aloha,
Jake

Jake Harmon · Atlanta, GA

jakebluesharmon@gmail.com
